title: Home
content:
Detect disease earlier. Make better care decisions. Protect health across species.

Advancing One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, and infectious disease preparedness.

The Aesculapius Foundation helps veterinary professionals, animal health workers, students, farms, shelters, caregivers, and communities use reliable knowledge and evidence-informed diagnostics to improve outcomes, reduce avoidable costs, and protect human, animal, and environmental health.

Support Our Mission
Resource Library
Explore Our Programs
Learn About Product Evaluation

The Aesculapius Foundation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit advancing One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, and infectious disease preparedness.

We support education, independent evaluation, surveillance readiness, and access-focused initiatives that help professionals and communities detect disease earlier, make better-informed decisions, reduce barriers to quality care, and respond to animal and public health threats.

Focused programs with practical value for donors, partners, professionals, and communities.

Independent Diagnostic Evaluation

Training, learning resources, and practical guidance that help veterinary and healthcare learners understand diagnostics, interpretation, workflow, and clinical value.

Veterinary Diagnostic Literacy

One Health education and preparedness initiatives focused on early detection, zoonotic risk awareness, infectious disease response, and animal health surveillance.

Access & Equity Equipment Grants

Non-commercial evaluation of diagnostic tools, consumables, workflows, and access-enabling technologies in real-world veterinary care settings.

One Health Education

Programs that help shelters, rural practices, farms, high-volume low-cost clinics, and animal owners access reliable information and reduce avoidable expense.

A veterinary diagnostic literacy and One Health resource library.

The Foundation is developing educational resources on diagnostic interpretation, infectious disease preparedness, animal health surveillance, evaluation methodology, workflow fit, total cost of ownership, and access-focused implementation support.

Resources will be evidence-informed, non-commercial, and designed for practical use by veterinary teams, farms, shelters, educators, students, donors, and community partners.

Explore the Resource Library
Editorial Independence

Resource Topics

  • Veterinary diagnostic literacy
  • One Health education
  • Infectious disease preparedness
  • Independent evaluation methodology
  • Shelter, HVLC, rural, farm, and mobile-practice support
  • Grant and access-readiness resources

A renewed mission with a sharper fundraising focus.

The Foundation has prior experience developing independent medical education and CME-related programming with accredited providers, faculty experts, and healthcare-sector partners. Its renewed work focuses on high-need areas where education and independent information can produce measurable value: diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, animal health surveillance, and access to quality care.

Why It Matters

Better health outcomes begin with earlier detection and better decisions. Independent education and evaluation can help practices, farms, shelters, and communities avoid poor purchasing decisions, reduce unnecessary testing and delays, and strengthen care capacity in cost-sensitive settings.

Support early detection, better decisions, and access to reliable care.

Your support helps fund educational resources, independent evaluation studies, infectious disease preparedness initiatives, and access programs for veterinary practices, farms, shelters, students, caregivers, and communities.

Support Our Mission
Evaluation Program
Grant Program

title: Mission
content:
Advancing One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, and access to reliable care.

Our Mission

The Aesculapius Foundation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit advancing veterinary diagnostic literacy, One Health education, and infectious disease preparedness.

We help veterinary professionals, animal health workers, students, farms, shelters, caregivers, and communities use reliable knowledge and evidence-informed diagnostics to detect disease earlier, improve care decisions, reduce avoidable costs, and protect human, animal, and environmental health.

Named in honor of Asclepius — the ancient symbol of healing shared across human and animal medicine — the Foundation builds on a history of healthcare education while focusing its renewed work on practical, measurable needs in diagnostics, surveillance readiness, and access to quality care.

Legacy & Experience

The Aesculapius Foundation builds on prior experience supporting accredited healthcare education, grant-supported educational programming, and professional meeting-based learning. Earlier programs involved collaboration with accredited continuing education partners and educational grant support from established healthcare and life sciences organizations.

Today, the Foundation applies that experience to veterinary diagnostic literacy, One Health education, infectious disease preparedness, and improved access to reliable diagnostic tools in underserved care settings.

Prior educational activities involved accredited education collaborators such as Medical Education Resources, Postgraduate Institute for Medicine, and Mount Sinai / Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

Prior educational programs received grant support from healthcare and life sciences organizations including Abbott Immunology, Allergan, Allos Therapeutics, Artes Medical, Bioform Medical Inc., bioMérieux, Bristol Myers Squibb, Celgene, Centocor Inc., Eisai, Gilead, Merz Aesthetics, Onyx Pharmaceuticals, Pfizer, and Upside Endeavors.

Prior educational activities were conducted in connection with major professional meetings and clinical education settings, including AASLD, ASBMT, ASH, ASCO, CHEST, IDSA / ICAAC, the San Antonio Breast Cancer Symposium, the American Academy of Dermatology, the American Academy of Facial Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, the American Board of Facial Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, the American Society of Plastic Surgeons, and the American Association of Plastic Surgeons.

Organizational names are provided to describe historical educational program experience. Inclusion does not imply current sponsorship, endorsement, partnership, or participation in The Aesculapius Foundation’s current programs.

Supporters & Technology Resources

The One Health Framework

The Foundation embraces One Health: the recognition that human health, animal health, and environmental health are inseparably connected. This framework is especially important where animal health surveillance, zoonotic disease awareness, food and farm systems, companion animal care, and community health intersect.

By working across human and veterinary health education, the Foundation aims to strengthen early detection, responsible diagnostic use, outbreak preparedness, and informed decision-making in communities of every kind.

What We Stand For

Our work is guided by independence, practical value, and the belief that better information can improve outcomes and reduce avoidable barriers to care.

Educational resources grounded in credible evidence, rigorous inquiry, transparent methodology, and responsible communication.

Clear, practical education on how diagnostic tools and related technologies work, what they measure, what they mean, and how they should inform clinical and operational decisions.

Education and collaboration that support early detection, animal health surveillance, zoonotic risk awareness, and community-based response capacity.

Lifelong learning, ethical practice, clinical education, and professional development for health and veterinary professionals.

Programs that help practices, farms, shelters, and care communities access reliable information, make better purchasing and care decisions, and reduce unnecessary expense.

Our Commitment

The Aesculapius Foundation is committed to developing educational initiatives that are practical, accessible, independent, and aligned with the real needs of healthcare and veterinary professionals. As the Foundation grows, its work includes diagnostic literacy resources, independent evaluation and product evaluation studies, infectious disease preparedness education, professional development, community health literacy initiatives, and partnerships with mission-aligned organizations.

The Foundation does not provide medical or veterinary diagnosis, treatment, or individualized clinical advice. Our role is educational: to help professionals and communities access reliable information and strengthen their capacity for informed decision-making.

title: Programs
content:
Focused, fundable initiatives that support earlier detection, better decisions, and access to reliable care.

Program Areas

The Aesculapius Foundation has a history of developing and supporting independent healthcare education programs. After a period of dormancy, the Foundation has reactivated its mission with a sharper focus on One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, independent evaluation, and access-focused support for underserved or cost-sensitive care settings.

For fundraising and program clarity, the Foundation organizes its work around three core pillars.

Training, learning resources, micro-credentials, webinars, decision-support guides, and professional development that help veterinary and healthcare learners understand diagnostic performance, interpretation, workflow fit, and practical clinical value.

One Health education and preparedness initiatives focused on animal health surveillance, zoonotic disease awareness, early warning concepts, infectious disease response training, and community-based detection capacity.

Programs that help shelters, high-volume low-cost clinics, rural and mobile practices, farms, animal owners, and other care communities access reliable information, diagnostic tools, implementation support, and education that can reduce avoidable expense.

Representative Program Concepts

Future program development may include a Veterinary Diagnostic Literacy Hub, One Health surveillance training, infectious disease response simulations, evidence-informed diagnostics fellowships, youth and student education, professional micro-credentials, rural and farm outreach, and a global One Health knowledge exchange.

These concepts will be developed selectively based on funding, partnerships, public benefit, and alignment with the Foundation's educational and charitable purposes.

Program principles

  • Independent and educational
  • Evidence-informed and non-commercial
  • Focused on practical decisions and measurable value
  • Relevant to professionals, farms, shelters, and communities
  • Aligned with One Health and public benefit

How We Work

All Foundation programs maintain independence from commercial interests. We do not promote specific products, vendors, or brands. Manufacturer, distributor, sponsor, or partner participation in evaluation or education initiatives does not control Foundation findings, editorial content, methodology, or dissemination.

Content is grounded in credible evidence, responsible communication, transparent methodology, and the real-world needs of professionals and communities.

Interested in participating or partnering?

Contact us about sponsoring a program area, participating in independent evaluation, supporting access-focused grants, or building a mission-aligned educational partnership.

Support Our Mission
Program Intake
Contact Us

title: Diagnostic Evaluation Program
content:
Independent. Non-commercial. Practitioner-centered. Focused on real-world value.

What It Is

The Aesculapius Foundation's Diagnostic Evaluation Program provides independent, non-commercial evaluation of veterinary point-of-care diagnostic instruments and related technologies, consumables, workflows, and access-enabling tools. Studies and reviews are conducted in real-world veterinary practice settings using standardized, transparent protocols. Findings are published and made available to the veterinary community without charge.

Diagnostic instruments remain the program's flagship focus. As veterinary practices increasingly rely on connected systems, consumables, sample-handling tools, software workflows, and access-oriented equipment, the Foundation may also evaluate adjacent products where independent, practice-level assessment would benefit practitioners, educators, farms, shelters, animal owners, and underserved care settings.

This program fills a gap that manufacturers, distributors, and trade publications cannot fill: truly independent, practice-level performance, usability, workflow, and value data on the tools veterinary teams actually use.

What We Evaluate

  • Veterinary point-of-care diagnostic instruments and systems
  • Analytical accuracy compared to established reference methods
  • Precision and reproducibility across operators, shifts, and time
  • Testing supplies, reagents, consumables, cartridges, and sample-handling materials
  • Workflow integration, staff learning curve, setup, maintenance, and daily usability
  • Connectivity, reporting, software, and practice-management workflow considerations
  • Total cost of ownership, consumable burden, service requirements, and implementation costs
  • Result turnaround time, reliability, and clinical utility in live practice settings
  • Field-use suitability for shelters, HVLC clinics, rural practices, mobile services, farms, and other resource-constrained environments

Who Benefits

Access independent performance, usability, and value data before making equipment or workflow decisions.

Use validated, non-commercial data in curriculum and professional development materials.

Benefit when better diagnostic decisions reduce delays, unnecessary expense, and barriers to timely care.

Independent U.S. practice-level evaluation data can become a credible third-party performance record while preserving educational integrity.

Access tools, implementation guidance, and validated data designed for high-throughput, resource-constrained settings.

Program Tracks

The Foundation may organize evaluations into focused tracks, including diagnostic instrument evaluation, consumables and sample-handling evaluation, practice workflow and usability assessment, total cost and affordability assessment, access and field-use evaluation, and veterinary education or training resource review.

This broader structure allows the program to remain focused on diagnostics while also addressing related products and workflows that affect diagnostic quality, clinical decision-making, affordability, and access to care.

How to Participate

The program is now accepting applications. We welcome interest from veterinary practices interested in hosting an evaluation, manufacturers seeking independent U.S. performance or usability assessment, distributors interested in supporting an evaluation, and veterinary educators or organizations interested in partnering on research design or dissemination.

Use the application form below to describe your interest. The Foundation will follow up within five business days to discuss fit, timing, study design, and any participation requirements.

Evaluation Program — Interest & Application

The Aesculapius Foundation is now accepting applications from veterinary practices, manufacturers, distributors, and veterinary educators interested in participating in or sponsoring independent diagnostic evaluation or related product evaluation studies.

Applications are submitted securely through Google Forms and logged in the Foundation's response system.

Submit Your Application
Equipment Grants
Contact Us

The application form opens in a new tab on Google Forms. If you have an urgent inquiry, contact SoniaTouby@aesculapiusfoundation.org.

A Note on Independence

Manufacturer or distributor participation in an evaluation study does not influence study design, methodology, data collection, analysis, or findings. The Foundation maintains full editorial and methodological independence. Participants who sponsor or facilitate a study receive no preferential treatment in findings, no right of review prior to publication, and no ability to restrict dissemination of results.

This independence is the foundation of the program's value — to practitioners, to the profession, to communities seeking reliable care, and to manufacturers or partners whose products perform well under honest scrutiny.

Interested in supporting or co-funding evaluation studies? Learn about partnership opportunities.

title: Access, Affordability & Equipment Grant Program
content:
Helping qualified organizations expand access to diagnostic tools, veterinary supplies, educational resources, and practical implementation support.

Program Overview

The Aesculapius Foundation's Access, Affordability & Equipment Grant Program is designed to support qualified organizations that serve underserved animals, communities, farms, or veterinary care settings. Through this program, eligible applicants may request donated or grant-supported diagnostic equipment, supplies, testing materials, educational resources, or implementation support that align with the Foundation's mission.

The goal is not simply to lower cost. The goal is to improve access to reliable care by helping organizations use diagnostics, supplies, and education in ways that support earlier detection, better care decisions, reduced avoidable expense, and stronger community health capacity.

Requests are reviewed case by case. Submission of a request does not guarantee approval. Awards depend on available funding, donated equipment, partner support, program priorities, and eligibility review.

Who May Apply

The program is intended for organizations whose work expands access to veterinary care, diagnostic capacity, disease preparedness, or community health education.

Organizations providing direct care, triage, disease prevention, or placement services for animals in need.

High-volume low-cost veterinary clinics serving communities with limited access to care.

Veterinary teams serving rural, farm, mobile, remote, or resource-limited settings.

Veterinary schools, teaching programs, or training organizations with mission-aligned needs.

Organizations addressing animal health, infectious disease preparedness, community health, or One Health access gaps.

What Organizations May Request

  • Veterinary diagnostic instruments or point-of-care testing tools
  • Testing supplies, consumables, or sample-handling materials
  • Veterinary medical supplies aligned with access-to-care goals
  • Educational materials, training resources, or implementation support
  • Surveillance, early detection, or infectious disease preparedness resources
  • Other mission-aligned equipment or supply needs

Review Criteria

Requests may be evaluated based on community need, population served, organizational capacity, intended use, ability to maintain and responsibly use equipment, expected impact, affordability benefit, and alignment with the Foundation's charitable and educational purposes.

Important: Grant support is not an entitlement and should not be represented as guaranteed funding, free equipment, or automatic approval.

Submit a Grant Request

Qualified organizations may submit a formal grant request through the Foundation's Access, Affordability & Equipment Grant Request form. The form collects organization details, nonprofit or service status, population served, requested equipment or supplies, intended use, need statement, expected impact, affordability benefit, and maintenance capacity.

Requests are submitted securely through Google Forms and logged in the Foundation's response system.

Submit a Grant Request
Support This Program

The grant request form opens in a new tab on Google Forms. If you have an urgent inquiry, contact SoniaTouby@aesculapiusfoundation.org.

Program Independence and Stewardship

The Foundation is committed to responsible stewardship of donated funds, equipment, supplies, and partner-supported resources. Grant decisions are made in alignment with the Foundation's mission and charitable purposes. When equipment or supplies are provided through partner support, the Foundation will seek to preserve appropriate independence, transparency, and educational integrity.

Interested in supporting or co-funding access programs? Learn about partnership opportunities.

title: Advisory Board
content:
Help guide One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, independent evaluation, and access-oriented programs.

About the Advisory Board

The Aesculapius Foundation is building an advisory board of respected professionals and community leaders who can strengthen the Foundation's educational mission, program quality, independence, public credibility, and fundraising readiness.

Advisory board members may provide subject-matter guidance, review educational priorities, help identify community and practice needs, support appropriate introductions, and advise on programs related to One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, independent evaluation, professional development, access, affordability, and community impact.

Advisory board service is intended to be consultative. It is not a corporate board director position unless separately approved and documented by the Foundation.

Who Should Apply

The Foundation welcomes interest from people with relevant expertise, credibility, or community reach.

  • Veterinarians, veterinary specialists, technicians, and practice leaders
  • Human healthcare professionals, educators, and researchers
  • Diagnostic medicine, laboratory, or point-of-care technology experts
  • One Health, public health, shelter medicine, infectious disease, or access-to-care leaders
  • Farm, livestock, rural health, nonprofit, philanthropic, academic, regulatory, or community partners
  • Professionals who can help translate complex health information into practical education

What Advisors May Help With

Advise on educational priorities, evaluation topics, surveillance and preparedness themes, outreach strategy, and program relevance.

Lend practical perspective and help ensure that Foundation work serves real practice, farm, shelter, and community needs.

Comment on educational materials, public-facing resources, study concepts, or access initiatives.

Where appropriate, connect the Foundation with mission-aligned partners, educators, practices, farms, organizations, donors, or community leaders.

Independence and Conflicts

The Foundation maintains educational, editorial, and methodological independence. Advisory board members are asked to disclose relevant professional, financial, commercial, institutional, or personal relationships that could create an actual, potential, or perceived conflict of interest.

Manufacturer, distributor, sponsor, donor, or partner relationships do not automatically disqualify a candidate, but transparency is required. Advisors may be recused from specific discussions where a conflict exists.

After You Apply

The Foundation will review advisory board interest submissions for mission alignment, expertise, potential conflicts, and current program needs. Selected candidates may be invited to an introductory conversation and, if there is mutual fit, asked to complete an advisory board agreement, conflict-of-interest disclosure, and public bio consent form.

Apply: The advisory board application form is being rebuilt as a native WordPress form (Phase 5 — forms & donations). In the meantime, email SoniaTouby@aesculapiusfoundation.org with your background, area of expertise, and interest in serving.

title: Support Our Mission
content:
Help professionals and communities detect disease earlier, make better care decisions, and expand access to reliable diagnostics and education.

Why Support the Foundation

The Aesculapius Foundation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit advancing One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, independent evaluation, and access-focused support for underserved or cost-sensitive care settings.

Support helps fund practical educational resources, independent evaluation and product evaluation studies, disease preparedness initiatives, and access programs for veterinary practices, farms, shelters, students, caregivers, animal owners, and communities.

Donors and partners can help close a real gap: professionals and care communities need trustworthy, independent information about diagnostics, surveillance, workflow, cost, and clinical value before decisions are made.

Support may help fund learning resources, webinars, toolkits, micro-credentials, and decision-support guides that make diagnostic knowledge easier to understand and apply.

Support may help develop One Health education, zoonotic disease awareness resources, early detection training, infectious disease response simulations, and community-based preparedness programs.

Support may help make non-commercial diagnostic evaluation data available to veterinary practices, educators, shelters, farms, and underserved care settings without charge.

Support may help expand diagnostic education, equipment access, implementation guidance, and cost-sensitive care initiatives for shelters, high-volume low-cost clinics, rural practices, mobile services, farms, and animal owners.

Ways to Support

The Foundation expects most major support to come through check, ACH, wire transfer, corporate or manufacturer support, restricted program grants, and in-kind contributions rather than small online donations.

For ACH, wire, check instructions, restricted program gifts, corporate sponsorship, manufacturer-supported evaluation studies, equipment donations, software credits, educational sponsorships, or in-kind support, please contact us.

Request Giving Instructions
Contact Us
Resource Library

Support Channels

  • Check, ACH, or wire contribution
  • Corporate or manufacturer charitable support
  • Restricted program support for education, evaluation, preparedness, or access
  • Equipment, consumables, software, cloud credits, or services
  • Educational sponsorships and reviewer support
  • Institutional grants and partner-supported programs

Contributions may be tax-deductible to the extent permitted by law. Donor acknowledgment and any restricted-use terms should be documented in writing.

Program-Aligned Support

Support may be directed generally to the Foundation's charitable and educational purposes or discussed in relation to a specific program area, subject to Foundation acceptance and stewardship requirements.

  • Veterinary Diagnostic Literacy & Education
  • One Health Education and Infectious Disease Preparedness
  • Diagnostic Evaluation Program
  • Access, Affordability & Equipment Grant Program
  • Resource Library development and reviewer participation

Transparency

The Aesculapius Foundation maintains a Candid Gold profile, reflecting our commitment to nonprofit transparency and accessible organizational information.

View Our Candid Gold Profile

Independence and Stewardship

Foundation support does not provide product endorsement, editorial control, preferential findings, or sponsor control over validation methodology, educational content, grant decisions, or publication decisions.

The Foundation maintains educational, editorial, and methodological independence and will seek appropriate transparency for donor, sponsor, manufacturer, distributor, or partner support.

Editorial Independence
Evaluation Program
Grant Program

Contact Us

Educational Purpose

The Aesculapius Foundation provides educational resources and does not provide medical or veterinary diagnosis, treatment, or individualized clinical advice.

title: Resource Library
content:
Veterinary diagnostic literacy, One Health education, infectious disease preparedness, evaluation methodology, and access-focused guidance.

Building a Practical Knowledge Center

The Aesculapius Foundation is developing a public resource library to help veterinary professionals, animal health workers, students, farms, shelters, high-volume low-cost clinics, rural and mobile practices, animal owners, and community partners make better-informed decisions.

Resources will be educational, evidence-informed, non-commercial, and aligned with the Foundation's mission to advance One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, independent evaluation, and access to reliable care.

This library is educational only. It does not provide medical or veterinary diagnosis, treatment, or individualized clinical advice.

Plain-language explainers on diagnostic performance, interpretation, workflow fit, clinical utility, limitations, total cost of ownership, and practical decision-making.

Educational resources on the connection between human health, animal health, environmental health, farms, shelters, communities, and early detection.

Guidance on animal health surveillance, zoonotic disease awareness, outbreak readiness, field-use diagnostics, and community-based detection capacity.

Resources explaining how independent diagnostic evaluation, product evaluation, workflow assessment, usability review, and cost analysis should be designed.

Practical materials for resource-constrained care settings that need reliable information, implementation guidance, and access-oriented diagnostic support.

Checklists and guidance for organizations seeking equipment, supplies, education, evaluation participation, or access-focused implementation support.

Initial Topics in Development

  • What veterinary diagnostic literacy means and why it matters
  • How point-of-care diagnostics can support earlier detection
  • Understanding sensitivity, specificity, and predictive value in practice
  • Why independent evaluation matters for veterinary diagnostic tools
  • How workflow and staff usability affect diagnostic quality
  • Diagnostic readiness for shelters, farms, rural practices, and mobile care
  • Grant readiness for organizations requesting diagnostic equipment or supplies

Contributor and Reviewer Interest

The Foundation welcomes interest from veterinarians, veterinary technicians, educators, researchers, public health professionals, shelter medicine leaders, rural practice leaders, farm and livestock experts, and other qualified reviewers.

Reviewers may help strengthen accuracy, practical relevance, independence, and responsible communication.

Apply to Advise
Contact Us

Editorial Standards

Foundation resources should be useful, careful, independent, and transparent. As the library grows, articles and guides will identify their educational purpose, intended audience, sources or basis for statements, review status, last-updated date, and any relevant support or conflict disclosures.

Manufacturer, distributor, sponsor, donor, or partner participation will not control Foundation editorial content, methodology, conclusions, or dissemination.

Read Our Editorial Independence Statement
Validation Program

Help build an independent educational resource for the veterinary and One Health community.

Support can help fund educational guides, reviewer participation, evaluation resources, grant-readiness materials, and access-focused implementation support.

Support the Library
Discuss Partnership

title: Partner With Us
content:
Collaborate with the Foundation to advance independent evaluation, One Health education, and access to reliable care.

Why Partner With the Foundation

The Aesculapius Foundation is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit advancing One Health education, veterinary diagnostic literacy, infectious disease preparedness, and access to reliable care. Our programs are grounded in independence, transparency, and practical value for veterinary practices, farms, shelters, students, caregivers, and communities.

We welcome mission-aligned collaboration with manufacturers, distributors, veterinary practices, educational institutions, professional associations, community organizations, farms, shelters, and funding partners. Effective partnerships help the Foundation expand its reach while preserving the independence that makes its work credible and valuable to the professionals and communities it serves.

Ways to Partner

Manufacturers and distributors may support or participate in the Foundation's independent diagnostic evaluation and product evaluation program. Partner-supported studies follow the same non-commercial protocols as Foundation-initiated work. Partners receive no influence over study design, findings, or dissemination — and gain credible, practitioner-centered third-party performance data.

Organizations may donate diagnostic instruments, veterinary supplies, consumables, or other equipment to support the Foundation's Access, Affordability & Equipment Grant Program. Donated resources are distributed to qualified shelters, rural practices, high-volume low-cost clinics, farms, and other underserved care settings.

Accredited education providers, professional associations, veterinary schools, and community health organizations may collaborate on diagnostic literacy resources, infectious disease preparedness education, and One Health programming. All educational content maintains Foundation editorial and methodological independence.

Corporations, foundations, and individual donors may support specific program areas — including independent evaluation studies, access grants, educational resource development, and disease preparedness initiatives — through charitable contributions or designated grants.

What Partners Can Expect

  • Clear documentation of partnership terms, scope, and independence requirements
  • No editorial or methodological influence over Foundation findings or educational content
  • Transparent public acknowledgment of partner support, consistent with Foundation policy
  • Direct access to Foundation staff for program coordination and scheduling
  • Opportunities to reach veterinary practices, farms, shelters, educators, and care communities through Foundation programs
  • Alignment with a credentialed 501(c)(3) nonprofit with a Candid Gold transparency seal

Independence Is the Foundation's Value

The Foundation's usefulness to practitioners, educators, and communities depends on its independence from commercial interests. All partnership structures — including evaluation study participation, equipment donations, educational collaborations, and funding support — are designed to preserve that independence.

Partners whose products, programs, or resources perform well under honest, transparent evaluation gain something no marketing effort can replicate: credible, practitioner-centered evidence that practitioners, educators, and communities can trust.

Start a Partnership Conversation

The Foundation welcomes preliminary inquiries from mission-aligned organizations. Whether you are interested in participating in a validation study, donating equipment, supporting a program area, or exploring a broader educational collaboration, we would like to hear from you.

Contact Us
Validation Program
Equipment Grants

For partnership inquiries, contact contact@aesculapiusfoundation.org or use the contact page. The Foundation will follow up within five business days.

title: Supporters & Technology Resources
content:
Recognition of nonprofit technology programs, in-kind resources, and prior charitable support that help reduce costs and expand the Foundation's educational, scientific, and access-oriented work.

Recognition for transparency and appreciation

The Aesculapius Foundation is grateful for nonprofit technology programs, in-kind resources, and prior charitable support that help reduce operating costs and expand our ability to deliver educational, scientific, and access-oriented programs.

Current technology resources may include software credits, platform access, cloud tools, design tools, AI-assisted productivity tools, communications tools, and other nonprofit infrastructure support used for administration, education, research coordination, communications, and program development.

Recognition on this page is provided for transparency and appreciation only. It does not imply current sponsorship, endorsement, partnership, control over Foundation activities, approval of Foundation findings, or any right to influence educational content, diagnostic evaluation, publication standards, governance, or program decisions.

Technology and infrastructure resources

The Foundation may use a range of nonprofit-access technology resources to support administration, communications, education, research coordination, and program development.

Software, cloud, and productivity tools

Nonprofit software access, cloud credits, productivity tools, design tools, AI-assisted workflow tools, and communications resources help reduce administrative cost and improve program execution.

Program infrastructure support

In-kind equipment, storage, logistics, supplies, and related infrastructure may support educational programming, diagnostic-literacy activities, and access-oriented deployment.

Recognition after verification

Organization names or logos should be listed only when the support is active or historically verified, and logos should be used only when the provider's brand-use rules permit public acknowledgment.

Historical supporters and prior contributors

The Foundation also recognizes organizations and individuals that supported earlier educational, scientific, or charitable activities.

Historical recognition reflects prior support and does not imply current funding, sponsorship, endorsement, partnership, or active affiliation.

Prior educational program experience and grant support are described on the Foundation's mission page for historical context.

View Mission and Legacy

Use of names and logos

Use of any company name or logo is subject to applicable grant terms, donor terms, brand guidelines, and Foundation approval. The Foundation does not permit supporter recognition to be used in a way that suggests commercial endorsement, preferred-vendor status, control over Foundation programs, or influence over independent evaluation or publication standards.

Supporter recognition is appreciation-only and does not create governance rights, advisory authority, product endorsement, exclusivity, publication control, or a commercial relationship unless separately documented in writing.

Interested in supporting Foundation infrastructure?

Companies and donors may support the Foundation through charitable contributions, in-kind technology access, equipment donations, consumable donations, or grant-in-aid support aligned with the Foundation's educational and access-oriented mission.

Support Our Mission
Contact Us

title: Editorial Independence
content:
Independent, educational, evidence-informed, and non-commercial standards for Foundation resources and programs.

Our Commitment

The Aesculapius Foundation maintains editorial, educational, and methodological independence in its public resources, evaluation work, grant programs, advisory activities, and public communications.

Our role is educational. We do not provide medical or veterinary diagnosis, treatment, or individualized clinical advice. We aim to help professionals, students, farms, shelters, animal owners, and communities access reliable information and strengthen informed decision-making.

The Foundation does not promote specific products, vendors, brands, manufacturers, or distributors as part of its educational mission.

Study design, protocols, data collection, analysis, findings, and dissemination must remain under Foundation control or under independently governed program standards.

Manufacturer, distributor, sponsor, donor, or partner support should be disclosed when relevant to a resource, project, evaluation, or program.

External support does not provide control over Foundation findings, editorial content, conclusions, publication decisions, or educational recommendations.

Foundation materials should distinguish evidence, interpretation, limitations, practical guidance, and areas where professional judgment is required.

Advisors, reviewers, contributors, and partners are expected to disclose relevant relationships that may create actual, potential, or perceived conflicts.

Evaluation

For diagnostic evaluation work, independence is essential to the program's value. Manufacturer or distributor participation may support access to devices, materials, training, data exports, or technical information, but does not control study design, findings, or dissemination.

Where manufacturer or partner support exists, the Foundation should disclose the nature of that support in a manner appropriate to the project.

Evaluation Standards

  • Clear evaluation purpose
  • Transparent methodology
  • Defined setting and intended use
  • Disclosure of material support
  • Balanced discussion of limitations
  • Publication free from sponsor control

Educational Resources

Foundation resources should be practical, readable, and grounded in credible evidence or transparent expert review. As the resource library grows, materials may include intended audience, sources, review status, last-updated date, and disclosure notes where relevant.

Resources are designed to support understanding and decision-making. They are not a substitute for professional veterinary, medical, public health, legal, financial, or regulatory advice.

Donors, Sponsors, and Partners

The Foundation welcomes mission-aligned support from individuals, corporations, manufacturers, distributors, educational institutions, farms, shelters, practices, public agencies, and community organizations. Support does not entitle a donor or partner to favorable findings, endorsement, product promotion, or editorial control.

Program participation and support are evaluated for alignment with the Foundation's charitable and educational purposes, public benefit, independence, and stewardship responsibilities.
